sessions.title: The Nature of Communication
indicators: Explain what communication is
Describe the communication process
Write down the different types of communication
sessions.content:
What is Communication?

Communication is the exchange of information from one person to another through a medium.

Key definitions:

  • Richard Nordquist: Communication is the process of sending and receiving messages through verbal or non-verbal means, including speech (oral communication), writing and graphical representations, and signs, signals, and behavior
  • James Carey: Communication is a 'symbolic process whereby reality is produced, maintained, repaired and transformed' – we define our reality by sharing our experience with others

The Communication Process

Key elements:

  • Sender: The person who has something to say to someone
  • Receiver/Recipient: The person receiving the information
  • Message: The information to be delivered to the receiver
  • Channel/Route/Medium: How the information is to be sent (letter, email, phone call, face-to-face conversation, Skype)
  • Feedback: The response the sender gets from the person who has received the information
  • Interference/Barrier: Anything that blocks the reception of the message or feedback (e.g., noise on a phone call)
  • Context: The time or place communication occurs

Process flow:

  • The message is encoded by the sender and sent through a channel to the recipient
  • The recipient decodes the message and sends feedback to the sender
  • The sender decodes the feedback for the communication process to be complete

Types of Communication

1. Verbal Communication – Using words.

  • Intrapersonal Communication: Extremely private and restricted to ourselves – the silent conversations we have with ourselves (thinking, processing thoughts)
  • Interpersonal Communication: Takes place between two individuals – one-on-one conversation
  • Small Group Communication: More than two people involved – small enough to allow each participant to interact (press conferences, board meetings, team meetings)
  • Public Communication: One individual addresses a large gathering (election campaigns, public speeches)

2. Non-Verbal Communication

  • Communication without using words or sounds
  • Uses gestures, body language, facial expressions, eye contact, clothing, tone of voice
  • Acts as a 'spice' to add flavour when communicating

3. Written Communication

  • Messages conveyed with the help of written words
  • Examples: letters, personal journals, e-mails, reports, articles, memos
  • Can be edited and rectified before being sent

4. Visual Communication

  • Visual display of information – message is understood or expressed with the help of visual aids
  • Examples: topography, photography, signs, symbols, maps, colours, posters, banners, designs
  • Includes movies, plays, television shows, video clips, internet

sessions.title: Barriers to Effective Communication
indicators: Explain the terms Active Listening, Clarification and Reflection in communication
List at least five barriers to effective communication
Describe five ways these barriers can be removed
sessions.content:
Barriers to Effective Communication

Active Listening: When people involved in the communication process carefully pay attention to what the other person is saying.

Clarification: Asking questions to be sure that what you have heard is correct.

Reflection: The act of participants carefully thinking through what has been conveyed so they can give meaningful feedback.

Common Barriers to Effective Communication

  • Use of jargon: Over-complicated or unfamiliar terms
  • Emotional barriers and taboos
  • Lack of attention, interest, distractions, or irrelevance to the receiver
  • Differences in perception and viewpoint
  • Physical disabilities: Hearing problems, speech difficulties
  • Physical barriers to non-verbal communication
  • Language differences and difficulty in understanding unfamiliar accents
  • Expectations and prejudices: False assumptions or stereotyping – people often hear what they expect to hear
  • Cultural differences: Norms of social interaction vary greatly in different cultures
  • Technical challenges: Noise on telephone, poor sound quality

How to Overcome Barriers to Effective Communication

1. Listen Actively

  • Fully concentrate on what is being said rather than just 'hearing'
  • Listen with all senses
  • Non-verbal cues: Smiling (if appropriate), making eye contact, nodding at appropriate times, avoiding distractions
  • Verbal signs: Reflecting, asking clarifying questions, paraphrasing

2. Use Simple Language

  • Know your audience and use language that can be easily understood
  • Avoid using medical terminologies or jargon when speaking to clients and their families
  • Pause occasionally and ask questions to ensure your message is being understood

3. Give Constructive Feedback

  • Feedback may occasionally be negative but must be constructive in nature
  • The intent should be to further the abilities of the speaker
  • Strengthens the interpersonal relationship and enhances future communications

sessions.title: Reading
indicators: Identify the three methods of reading
Indicate when to use which type of reading skill in their studies
sessions.content:
What is Reading?

Reading is the process of looking at a series of written symbols and getting meaning from them. When we read, we use our eyes to receive written symbols (letters, punctuation marks, and spaces) and our brains to convert them into words, sentences, and paragraphs that communicate something to us.

Types of Reading

1. Skimming

  • The process of quickly viewing a section of a text to get a general impression of the author's main argument, themes, or ideas

When to use skimming:

  • Selecting a book
  • Surveying a chapter before reading or studying
  • Finding appropriate material for use in research
  • Sorting through correspondence before answering it

2. Scanning

  • Quickly locating specific information – words, numbers, names, ideas, or answers to specific study questions

Method for scanning:

  • Fix clearly in your mind what you are looking for
  • Anticipate how the information will look
  • Run your finger down the middle of the page or backwards and forwards across the page

Visual clues for scanning:

  • Names, places, titles: Look for capital letters
  • Distances or dates: Look for numbers or words like miles, metres, minutes, hours
  • Ideas: Anticipate the words that could be used to state it
  • Relationships: Look for clue words like 'thus', 'consequently', 'as a result'

3. In-depth/Intensive Reading

  • Used to gain deeper meaning and comprehension of a text
  • Used for researching detailed information for an assignment
  • Used to read difficult sections of a text

Strategies for Intensive Reading

1. The RAP Strategy (Good for textbook explanations and research articles)

  • Read (a paragraph or a section)
  • Ask yourself some questions about what you have just read
  • Put the answers in your own words (and make notes if you need them)

2. The RIDA Strategy (Relates to descriptive and narrative texts)

  • Read the text
  • Imagine the scene you have just read about
  • Describe it to yourself
  • Add more detail as you read

3. SQ3R

  • Survey: Skim through the material, noting headings, sub-headings, diagrams, graphs
  • Question: Ask yourself questions about the material while reading
  • Read: Read using a slower in-depth reading style – pause frequently to answer questions
  • Recite: Make notes from memory on the sections you have just read
  • Review: Check your recalled notes against the section – add anything important missed

sessions.title: Developing Critical and Analytic Reading
indicators: Explain what is meant by critical reading
Ask questions about the texts they are reading to find out what the main points of the text are
sessions.content:
What is Critical Reading?

Critical reading involves presenting a reasoned argument that evaluates and analyses what you have read. Being critical in an academic sense means advancing your understanding, not dismissing and therefore closing off learning.

Key points:

  • To read critically is to exercise your judgement about what you are reading – not taking everything you read at face value
  • You will be faced with the author's interpretation and opinion
  • Different authors will have different views
  • You should always examine what you are reading critically and look for limitations, omissions, inconsistencies, oversights, and arguments against what you are reading

As a critical reader, you should reflect on:

  • What the text says: Take notes, paraphrasing the key points in your own words
  • What the text describes: Use examples and compare/contrast with other writings on the subject
  • Interpretation of the text: Fully analyse the text and state a meaning for the text as a whole

Graphic and Semantic Organisers

Semantic maps (graphic organizers): Maps or webs of words that visually display the meaning-based connections between a word or phrase and a set of related words or concepts.

Steps to create a semantic map:

  • Pick a word you don't know from a text you are reading
  • Use a blank map or begin to draw a map
  • Place the word you don't know in the centre of the map
  • Pronounce the word (use an online dictionary with audio if necessary)
  • Read the text around the word to see if there are related words you can add
  • Use an online dictionary or thesaurus to look up the word and find a definition
  • Find words and phrases that fit with the meaning – select pictures/images or draw pictures
  • Add these words, phrases, or images to your semantic map
  • Read the text again, applying the meaning of the word to the text

Metacognition

Metacognition: Understanding how you learn – looking inward to examine how you learn and to judge which methods are effective.

Metacognitive Strategies:

  • Ask Questions: Whatever you read or are told, ask questions about it (e.g., What is the passage about? How do I understand it?)
  • Foster Self-Reflection: Critically analyse what you already know about the subject you are reading about and find out how your assumptions may influence your learning
  • Autonomous Learning: Read other materials that are a little difficult for you to challenge you and help you develop your metacognitive strategies
  • Find Mentors: Learn by interacting with peers who are slightly more advanced – observe the proficient use of skill by an experienced learner
  • Solve Problems with a Team: Group work also helps

sessions.title: Note Taking and Note Making
indicators: Tell the difference between taking notes and making notes
Take notes from a book you have read
Transfer those notes into well-organized notes for easy recall
sessions.content:
What is Note-Taking?

Note-taking is a way of recording important details from a source (book, article, lecture, or oral discussion). When taking notes, the writer records the essence of the information.

Note-taking vs Note-making:

  • Note-taking: Recording points as you hear or read them – like a recorder taking down points
  • Note-making: A careful arrangement of the information you have recorded – adding other relevant information from other books and sources – more involving and organized

Advantages of Note Making

  • Helps in keeping information handy whenever we need it
  • Helps in recollecting and recalling what we have heard or read
  • Serves as a source of reference
  • Helps in understanding the material better as the notes are in your own words
  • Helps us to distinguish between main points and supporting details of a topic

Do's of Note Taking

  • Be consistent – use a similar format each time
  • Always record your sources – date, subject, lecturer, title, author, page numbers
  • Use 'speech marks' or a different colour pen to identify direct quotations
  • Use abbreviations – but only if you'll remember what they stand for later
  • Keep separate notebooks/binders/folders for each course
  • Use colour, shape, and dotted lines to highlight, link, and group ideas

Don'ts of Note Taking

  • Don't copy word for word – note down what you think are the key points
  • Don't use 'pocket' notepads – use big notebooks to give yourself room
  • Don't rely on PowerPoint handouts – make your notes as well
  • Don't worry if you miss something – aim to get the big picture
  • Don't consider any examples too obvious – note them down
  • Don't write down more than you would want to read again

Methods of Making Notes

A. Diagrammatic Formats – Notes arranged in an artistic format with a particular order.

  • Outline – Skeletal pattern of a text
  • Mind maps/Spider gram
  • Matrix/Grid
  • Flow-charts
  • Herringbone notes

B. Un-diagrammatic Formats – Notes written in prose or block form with no fixed pattern.

  • Paraphrase: A written record in your own words of what you have read or heard
  • Summary: A brief paraphrase of an original work
  • Quotation
  • Headline

sessions.title: Writing the Outline
indicators: Explain what a topic sentence is
Distinguish between major supporting sentences and minor supporting sentences
Outline a passage
sessions.content:
What is an Outline?

An outline is a skeletal frame of a text. It shows the major and minor ideas in a piece of writing. The arrangements of the points in the outline should correspond exactly to the arrangements of the material in the text.

Key concepts:

  • Topic sentence: Usually found at the beginning of the paragraph – tells us exactly what the paragraph will be about
  • Major supporting sentences: Provide reasons/elaboration for the topic sentence – further explain the topic sentences
  • Minor supporting sentences: Give more information about a major supporting sentence

Outline Frame

Title of paper

I. Introduction

  • Central Idea (thesis statement)

II. Heading (topic sentence)

  • A. 1st Major support
    • i. Minor support
    • ii. Minor support
  • B. 2nd Major support
    • i. Minor support
    • ii. Minor support
  • C. Heading (topic sentence)
  • D. Heading (topic sentence)
  • E. Conclusion

sessions.title: Summary Writing
indicators: Explain what a summary is
Understand the steps involved in writing a summary
Write a summary of a passage following the steps taught
sessions.content:
What is a Summary?

A summary is a condensed form of the original material. The purpose of a summary is to give the reader, in about 1/3 of the original length of an article/lecture, a clear, objective picture of the original text.

Key point: The summary restates only the main points of a text or lecture without giving examples or details such as dates, numbers, or statistics.

Preparing to Write a Summary

  1. Skim the text – noting subheadings – determine what type of text you are dealing with
  2. Read the text – highlighting important information and taking notes
  3. In your own words, write down the main points of each section
  4. Write down the key support points for the main topic, but do not include minor detail
  5. Go through the process again, making changes as appropriate

Writing the Summary

  1. Write a one-sentence summary of each paragraph
  2. Formulate a single sentence that summarizes the whole text
  3. Write a paragraph (or more) – begin with the overall summary sentence and follow it with the paragraph summary sentences
  4. Rearrange and rewrite the paragraph to make it clear and concise
  5. The final version should be complete, unified, and coherent

Rules for Summary Writing

  • Use your own words
  • Remove all examples, figures, and parenthetical expressions
  • Significantly condense the original text
  • Provide accurate representations of the main points
  • Avoid personal opinion
  • Introduce the name of the author whose work you are summarizing
  • Introduce the title of the text being summarized

sessions.title: Basic Sentences
indicators: Identify and write a basic sentence
Identify the types of basic sentence structures available
Write sentences reflecting these structures
sessions.content:
What is a Sentence?

A sentence contains a subject and a verb. The simple sentence makes a statement, gives a command, expresses emotions, or asks a question.

Examples:

  • The sun sets in the West. (Statement)
  • Where is my chair? (Question)
  • I am so happy! (Emotion)
  • Go home! (Command)

Parts of a Sentence

  • Subject: The person, place, thing, or idea that is doing or being something
  • Verb: The action or state of being
  • Object: The receiver of the action (direct and indirect)
  • Complement: Completes the meaning of the subject or object
  • Adjunct/Adverbial: Provides additional information (time, place, manner)

Basic Sentence Structures

1. SV (Subject + Verb)

  • Verb is intransitive (does not take an object)
  • Examples: He cried. / The man sang. / The girl in blue danced.

2. SVO (Subject + Verb + Direct Object)

  • Verb is mono-transitive (takes one object)
  • Examples: Anthonia killed the snake. / Wofa Yaw praised the pupil.

3. SVOO (Subject + Verb + Indirect Object + Direct Object)

  • Verb is di-transitive (takes two objects)
  • Examples: He gave the toddler an orange. / My grandmother told all the little children stories.

4. SVC (Subject + Verb + Subject Complement)

  • Verb is intensive/linking/copular (e.g., be, seem, appear, look, become)
  • Subject complement is either a noun phrase or an adjective
  • Examples: The woman is married. / The pastor appears worried. / The groom looks handsome.

5. SVOC (Subject + Verb + Object + Object Complement)

  • Verb is complex transitive (e.g., make, prove, elect, appoint, choose, baptize, call, nominate)
  • Examples: The students elected Kwabena captain of the team. / The parents christened their daughter Adoma.

6. SVA (Subject + Verb + Adjunct/Adverbial)

  • Adjunct indicates place, time, or manner
  • Examples: The sisters live at Brafo Yaw. / The examiners are here.

7. SVOA (Subject + Verb + Object + Adjunct)

  • Verb is complex transitive – adjunct mainly of place
  • Examples: The workers pulled the unconscious man out of the gutter. / Kwadwo put the plates on the table.

sessions.title: Paragraph Writing
indicators: Write topic sentences for paragraphs
Identify the three characteristics of a good paragraph
Write a paragraph with a well-defined form
sessions.content:
What is a Paragraph?

A paragraph is a collection of sentences which all relate to one main idea or topic. Effective paragraphs have three main characteristics: unity, coherence, and adequate development.

Topic Sentences

Topic sentence: Usually the first sentence of the paragraph – captures the main idea in the paragraph. It describes what the paragraph will be about, allowing the reader to have clear expectations about what will follow.

Examples of topic sentences:

  • 'The cockroaches that inhabit many city apartments and homes are parasites that are almost impossible to exterminate completely.'
  • 'Many television cartoons contain an unhealthy amount of violence.'
  • 'An increasing number of people in America are enjoying the benefits of organically grown fruits and vegetables.'

Unity

  • Refers to the extent to which all ideas contained within a given paragraph 'hang together' in a way that is easy for the reader to understand
  • When the writer changes to a new idea (not consistent with the topic sentence), the writer should begin a new paragraph
  • Aids the reader in following along with the writer's ideas

Coherence

  • Refers to the extent to which the flow of ideas in a paragraph is easily understood by the reader
  • Closely related to unity – when a writer changes main ideas or topics within a paragraph, confusion often results
  • A writer should show how all ideas contained in a paragraph are relevant to the main topic

Adequate Development

  • A paragraph is adequately developed when it describes, explains, and supports the topic sentence
  • If the 'promise' of the topic sentence is not fulfilled, or if the reader is left with questions, the paragraph has not been adequately developed
  • Generally, a paragraph should contain between three and five sentences – all of which help clarify and support the main idea

sessions.title: Unit Writing
indicators: Plan how to write an essay
Make a draft of the essay
Revise the draft of the essay to correct all mistakes
Edit the revised draft
sessions.content:
The Writing Process

Writing is a recursive process that involves at least four distinct steps: prewriting, drafting, revising, and editing.

1. Prewriting

  • Any activity you do before you write a draft of your document
  • Includes: thinking, taking notes, talking to others, brainstorming, outlining, and gathering information (interviewing, researching in the library, assessing data)
  • Generating ideas is an activity that occurs throughout the writing process

2. Drafting

  • Occurs when you put your ideas into sentences and paragraphs
  • Concentrate on explaining and supporting your ideas fully
  • Begin to connect your ideas
  • Don't pay attention to spelling at this stage
  • This draft tends to be writer-centred – you telling yourself what you know and think about the topic

3. Revising

  • Read over your work and think more deeply about what your reader needs and expects
  • How much support will each idea need?
  • Which terms should be defined for these particular readers?
  • Is your organization effective?
  • Refine your writing – make each sentence as concise and accurate as possible
  • Make connections between ideas explicit and clear

4. Editing

  • Check for grammar, mechanics, and spelling
  • Don't edit your writing until the other steps in the writing process are complete
  • The last thing you should do before printing or handing in your paper is to spell-check it

sessions.title: Types of Writing
indicators: Identify the four main types of writing
Determine when to use what type of writing
Write essays reflecting each type of writing
sessions.content:
1. Expository Writing

  • Used to explain a concept – imparting information to a wider audience
  • Does not include the author's opinions – focuses on accepted facts about a topic
  • Includes statistics or other evidence

Examples of Expository Writing:

  • Textbooks
  • Articles
  • Recipes
  • News stories (not editorials or Op-Eds)
  • Business, technical, or scientific writing

2. Descriptive Writing

  • Often found in fiction, though can appear in nonfiction (memoirs, first-hand accounts, travel guides)
  • Painting a picture in words of a person, place, or thing for the audience
  • May employ metaphor or other literary devices
  • Describes the author's impressions via the five senses (what they hear, see, smell, taste, or touch)

Examples of Descriptive Writing:

  • Poetry
  • Journal/diary writing
  • Descriptions of Nature
  • Fictional novels or plays

3. Persuasive Writing

  • Main style of writing used in academic papers
  • Used to convince the audience of a position or belief
  • Contains the author's opinions and biases, as well as justifications and reasons
  • Any 'argumentative' essay should be in the persuasive style

Examples of Persuasive Writing:

  • Cover letters
  • Op-Eds and Editorial newspaper articles
  • Reviews of items
  • Letters of complaint
  • Advertisements
  • Letters of recommendation

4. Narrative Writing

  • Used in almost every longer piece of writing, whether fiction or nonfiction
  • Not just trying to impart information – trying to construct and communicate a story
  • Complete with characters, conflict, and settings

Examples of Narrative Writing:

  • Oral histories
  • Novels/Novellas
  • Poetry (especially epic sagas or poems)
  • Short Stories
  • Anecdotes

sessions.title: Punctuation Marks
indicators: Identify the common punctuation marks we have in English
Use the punctuation marks correctly in sentences
Correct sentences that have wrongly used punctuation marks
sessions.content:
14 Common Punctuation Marks

Period, Question mark, Exclamation point, Comma, Semicolon, Colon, Dash, Hyphen, Parentheses, Brackets, Braces, Apostrophe, Quotation marks, Ellipsis.

Sentence Endings

Period (.)

  • Placed at the end of declarative sentences
  • After many abbreviations (e.g., Dec. 6, 2008)

Question Mark (?)

  • Indicates a direct question
  • Example: 'When did Jane leave for the market?'

Exclamation Point (!)

  • Used to express a sudden outcry or add emphasis
  • Example: 'We've won!' screamed Jane.

Comma, Semicolon, and Colon

Comma (,)

  • Shows separation of ideas or elements within a sentence
  • Used in numbers, dates, and letter writing
  • Direct address: 'Thanks for all your help, John.'
  • Separation of complete sentences: 'We went to the movies, and then we went out to lunch.'
  • Separating lists: 'Suzi wanted the black, green, and blue dress.'

Semicolon (;)

  • Connects independent clauses
  • Shows a closer relationship between clauses than a period
  • Example: 'John was hurt; he knew she only said it to upset him.'

Colon (:)

  • After a word introducing a quotation, explanation, example, or series
  • Between independent clauses when the second explains the first
  • For emphasis
  • Non-grammatical uses: time, ratio, business correspondence

Dash and Hyphen

Dash (-)

  • Horizontal line that floats in the middle of a line of text
  • Used to indicate a range or a pause
  • Separates groups of words

Hyphen (-)

  • Used to join two or more words together into a compound term
  • Not separated by spaces
  • Examples: part-time, back-to-back, well-known

Brackets, Braces, and Parentheses

Brackets ([ ])

  • Squared-off notations used for technical explanations or to clarify meaning
  • Example: 'He [Mr. Jones] was the last person seen at the house.'

Braces ({ })

  • Used to contain two or more lines of text or list items
  • Show they are considered as a unit
  • Not common in most writing – seen in computer programming and mathematical expressions

Parentheses ( )

  • Curved notations used to contain further thoughts or qualifying remarks
  • Can be replaced by commas without changing meaning
  • Example: 'John and Jane (who were actually half-brother and sister) both have red hair.'

Apostrophe (')

  • Indicates omission of a letter or letters from a word
  • Indicates the possessive case
  • Indicates plurals of lowercase letters
  • Examples: 'I've seen that movie.' / 'Sara's dog bit the neighbour.' / 'Mind your p's and q's.'

Quotation Marks (" ")

  • Mark the beginning and end of a passage attributed to another and repeated word for word
  • Indicate meanings and the unusual or dubious status of a word
  • Example: 'Don't go outside,' she said.

Single Quotation Marks (' ')

  • Used most frequently for quotes within quotes
  • Example: 'Marie told the teacher, 'I saw Marc at the playground, and he said to me 'Bill started the fight,' and I believed him.''

Ellipsis (. . .)

  • Represents three full stops (or three asterisks)
  • Used to indicate an omission, especially of letters or words
  • Frequently used within quotations to jump from one phrase to another
  • Example: 'She began to count, 'One, two, three, four…' until she got to 10.'

sessions.title: Referencing and Documentation
indicators: Identify the different types of documentation styles available
Cite the sources they use in their work
Create bibliographies of their own
sessions.content:
What is Documentation?

A documentation style is a standard approach to the citation of sources that the author of a paper has consulted, abstracted, or quoted from. It prescribes methods for citing references within the text, providing a list of works cited at the end of the paper, and formatting headings and margins.

Why cite sources?

  • Gives credit where it's due
  • Allows your reader to locate the sources you have consulted
  • Your reader must be able to duplicate the research you have done

What to Document

  • Direct quotations
  • Paraphrases and summaries
  • Information and ideas that are not common knowledge or are not available in a standard reference work
  • Any borrowed material that might appear to be your own if there were no citation

How to Gather Information for Documenting Sources

  • Write down the most complete bibliographic information available for each source
  • Note the page numbers where information appears
  • Double-check quotations for accuracy
  • Put citations into your paper as you draft it
  • Add the source to your working bibliography

Which Style to Use

Choosing the appropriate documentation style may depend on:

  • The requirements of the particular course
  • The standard for the discipline in which you are studying
  • Your individual preference

Common documentation styles:

  • MLA style (Modern Language Association) – used in humanities
  • APA style (American Psychological Association) – used in social sciences
  • Chicago style – used in history and some social sciences

Bibliography / Works Cited

A bibliography or works cited is a list of all of the sources you have used (whether referenced or not) in the process of researching your work – it comes at the end of the essay.

What a bibliography should include:

  • The authors' names
  • The titles of the works
  • The names and locations of the companies that published your copies of the sources
  • The dates your copies were published
  • The page numbers of your sources (if they are part of multi-source volumes)

Example of a bibliographic entry:

  • Smith, Jacob. Understanding Reading. Malta: Springer Publishers, 2003.
  • Mensah, A. Akan Phonology. Ghana: Asuagya Publishers, 2007.
